frame_analyses.description: First instrument tip becomes visible at the lower left edge and task timing starts.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Controlled entry into the field; slight setup delay before both instruments are fully engaged.

frame_analyses.description: Needle is secured in the right driver and aligned toward the Penrose target.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Acceptable needle loading, though there are a few small regrasping adjustments before the first bite.

frame_analyses.description: The second bite is being completed across the slit and the suture line spans both marked targets.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Bite depth looks reasonably symmetric and the passes appear close to the intended marks.

frame_analyses.description: The suture has been pulled through and the limbs are separated to begin intracorporeal knot tying.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Some extra tail organization is needed before the first throw is formed.

frame_analyses.description: The first throw is being advanced and seated at the approximation point.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Wrap pattern is most consistent with a surgeon's knot; it seats without obvious tearing or avulsion.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is formed and advanced over the first throw.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Alternating-hand setup appears present, but there is some extra regrasping before the throw is tightened.

frame_analyses.description: The third throw is being tightened onto the knot stack to lock the construct.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final throw appears to lay down flat, though the sequence remains deliberate rather than efficient.

frame_analyses.description: Scissors are introduced and trimming of the suture tails begins.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting phase starts after a relatively long tail-management interval following the final throw.

frame_analyses.description: Both tails appear cut and the instruments withdraw with the knot still intact.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Endpoint reached with the Penrose still secured and no drain avulsion.

knot_assessments.notes: Most consistent with a right-hand-led double throw; moderate confidence because the camera angle partially obscures the wrap count.
Likely switched to left-hand lead for the second throw and advanced it without visible loosening of the first throw.
Likely switched back to right-hand lead for the final locking throw; knot stack remained stable during tail cutting.

technique_summary: The trainee completed the task with accurate-looking suture placement, atraumatic handling, and a final knot that appears flat and secure. The main limitation was efficiency: there were repeated tail-management and regrasping pauses during each throw, especially before the final throw and before introducing scissors. The three throws together consumed about 102 seconds (~72% of total task time), indicating a trainee who understands the task and can place the stitch accurately, but is still not fully efficient in organizing suture limbs, forming loops, and advancing throws without extra regrasping.
improvement_suggestions: Set the short and long limbs earlier before each throw to reduce regrasping and unnecessary tail organization time.
Use a more decisive needle drive on the second bite so the placement phase is shorter while maintaining the same target accuracy.
After the third throw, transition to scissors sooner and pre-position both tails for trimming to shorten the end phase.
strengths: Needle passes appear close to both target marks with no evidence of drain avulsion.
The final knot stack remains stable, the slit appears well approximated, and there is no obvious residual gapping after tightening.
Hand alternation appears preserved across throws and the rubric-required sequence (surgeon's knot then two alternating single throws) looks intact.

rubric_assessment.overall_judgment: Technically competent, accurate, and completed performance. Main deduction comes from efficiency rather than major technical errors. Trainee achieved the important endpoints: correct stitch placement, appropriate knot sequence, preserved hand switching, intact final closure, and no avulsion. The score was held down mainly by time spent during knot tying, not by a major breakdown in technique.
rubric_assessment.uncertainty_notes: Estimated video-based score, not an official proctored FLS score. Highest uncertainty is in the exact millimeter deviation estimate and in the exact wrap count of the first throw.
